response:
A

The term "exothermic" refers to any chemical reaction that releases net energy (typically as heat) to its surroundings. Among the given options, only a car engine relies on an internal combustion reaction (burning gasoline or diesel with oxygen) that is strongly exothermic; the released heat expands gases to perform mechanical work while also requiring a cooling system (radiator) precisely because excess thermal energy is produced. By contrast, a refrigerator moves heat from inside to outside via a refrigeration cycle whose indoor phase is endothermic (evaporative cooling), a frying pan is a passive heat-transfer tool whose source is an external burner or element, and a glass bottle has no inherent reaction or energy-releasing function. Thus the car engine is the only everyday device that visibly and continuously demonstrates the heat output characteristic of an exothermic process.
